coredata.dc:title: Fluorescent carbon dots sensor for highly sensitive detection of guanine
coredata.prism:copyright: Copyright © 2015 Elsevier B.V. All rights reserved.
coredata.dc:creator: Pang, Shengbin
Zhang, Yi
Wu, Chengke
Feng, Suling
coredata.dc:description: In the present work, unmodified carbon nanodots (CDs) can act as fluorescent probes for detection and measurement of guanine concentration. CDs were synthesized by using a facile one-step microwave-assisted method with dl-malic acid and ethylenediamine as the precursors. The fluorescence of CDs can be obviously quenched by copper(II) ions through nonradiative electron transfer. Meanwhile, due to the coordination of copper(II) ions with guanine, the fluorescence of the CDs was restored reversibly by the addition of guanine. Under optimized conditions, the prepared CDs demonstrate a linear range from 1.31×10−8 to 7.27×10−7 molL−1 with the detection limit of 0.67×10−8 molL−1. The new fluorescence system has been applied for the determination of guanine in urine and DNA samples with the recoveries from 98.1% to 103.0%.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon nanodots
Fluorescent probe
Cu2+
Guanine

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: articleinfo articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pglast pii piinorm pubdateend pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sectiontitle sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure table e-component body mmlmath affil appendices articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast primabst ref vitae

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Response of CDs to metal ions

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: TEM image (A), the corresponding particle size distribution histogram (B) and HRTEM image (C) of CDs.
XPS full scan spectrum (A) and C1s (B), O1s (C), N1s (D) spectra of the as-prepared CDs.
Fluorescence spectra (A) and photographs (B) of CDs at different excitation wavelengths. CDs: 0.1mgmL−1.

Change in fluorescence intensity (−ΔF/F 0) of CDs solutions in the presence of various metal ions (A) and Cu2+ ions with base (B). CDs: 0.1mgmL−1; all the base: 1.0mgmL−1; metal ions: 1.0×10−3 molL−1.

Fluorescence spectra and corresponding photographs (inset, right) of the CDs–Cu2+–guanine system. (a) Guanine+buffer; (b) guanine+Cu2+ +buffer; (c) CDs+Cu2+ +buffer; (d) CDs+Cu2+ +guanine+buffer; (e) CDs+guanine+buffer; (f) CDs. CDs: 0.1mgmL−1; Cu2+: 8.0×10−6 molL−1; guanine: 6.62×10−8 molL−1; Tris–HCl: pH 7; T =100°C; t =5.0min.

Absorption spectra of Cu2+–guanine system. Red line: Cu2+ +buffer; blue line: guanine+buffer; black line: guanine+Cu2+ +buffer; Cu2+: 8.0×10−6 molL−1; guanine: 6.62×10−8 molL−1; Tris–HCl: pH 7; T =100 °C; t =5.0min. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)
A linear relationship of ΔF versus the concentration of guanine. CDs: 0.1mgmL−1; Cu2+: 8.0×10−6 molL−1; guanine: from 1.31×10−8 to 7.27×10−7 molL−1; Tris–HCl: pH 7; T =100 °C; t =5.0min.
Schematic model of fluorescence turn “off” and “on” process of CDs–Cu2+–guanine system.

row.entry: Cr3+, cysteine, tyrosine, arginine, lysine, threonine, aspartic acid, adenine, uracil, thymine, and cytosine
K+, Na+, Ni2+, Mn2+, Zn2+, NO3 −

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Results for the determination of guanine in urine and DNA samples.

head.ce:title: Fluorescent carbon dots sensor for highly sensitive detection of guanine

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Henan Normal University, Xinxiang, Henan 453007, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Henan Normal University

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Laboratory Medicine, Xinxiang Medical University, Xinxiang, Henan 453003, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Laboratory Medicine, Xinxiang Medical University

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In the present work, unmodified carbon nanodots (CDs) can act as fluorescent probes for detection and measurement of guanine concentration. CDs were synthesized by using a facile one-step microwave-assisted method with dl-malic acid and ethylenediamine as the precursors. The fluorescence of CDs can be obviously quenched by copper(II) ions through nonradiative electron transfer. Meanwhile, due to the coordination of copper(II) ions with guanine, the fluorescence of the CDs was restored reversibly by the addition of guanine. Under optimized conditions, the prepared CDs demonstrate a linear range from 1.31×10−8 to 7.27×10−7 molL−1 with the detection limit of 0.67×10−8 molL−1. The new fluorescence system has been applied for the determination of guanine in urine and DNA samples with the recoveries from 98.1% to 103.0%.

ce:section.ce:para: Guanine (G) is one of the four fundamental nucleobases found in DNA, which play significant roles in the coding of genetic information. The abnormal change of guanine in organism indicates the mutation and deficiency of the immunity system and may reflect the presence of some diseases such as cancers and AIDS [1,2]. Therefore, the detection and quantitation of guanine in organism have become increasing important in the field of clinical medicine and biological science [3]. Up to date, strategies for determination of guanine in different sample matrices mainly include electrochemistry [2,4,5], chromatography [6,7] and capillary electrophoresis [8–10]. Electrochemical methods encounter the problem of low sensitivity, labor-intensive electrode procedures, the poor stability of electrode and irreversible adsorption on the electrode surface. Chromatography generally requires expensive and sophisticated equipment. Capillary electrophoresis has the weakness of low stability and poor reproducibility. In these regards, more methods for simple, sensitive and reproducible measurements of guanine are still urgently desired.

Fluorescent probe sensing strategies have received considerable attention because of their intrinsic advantages and considerable research space [11,12]. To our knowledge, there have been very few reports on fluorescent probes for detecting guanine. Li et al. [13] developed thioglycolic acid-capped CdTe nanoparticles as fluorescence probes for the determination of guanine with the detection limit of 8.0×10−8 molL−1. However, this method has some limitations such as complicated preparing process and high toxicity of nanoparticles, and low tolerance to uric acid and tryptophan. Our group [14] recently presented a ‘off/on’ fluorescent probe of Cu2+-nuclear fast red to detect guanine in serum and DNA samples with the detection limit of 1.9×10−8 molL−1. Unfortunately, the method also shows low tolerance of coexisting species, such as adenine and arginine. Consequently, it is still in high demand to develop simple, sensitive and selective fluorescent probes for guanine measurements in biologic samples.

Fluorescent carbon nanodots (CDs) have recently attracted much attention because of their excellent properties such as low cytotoxicity, good water-solubility, high chemical inertness, excellent photostability, facile functionalization, low cost, and being both excellent electron donors and excellent electron acceptors. Apart from the use of CDs for biological labeling and imaging [15], photocatalysis [16], and optoelectronic devices [17], CDs have been employed as fluorescent probes for biological and environment detection owing to their distinct optical features [18]. However, most of the CDs reported show low fluorescence quantum yields (often less than 10%) [19], which severely restricts their use in practical applications. On the other hand, diversifying the surface group of CDs to enlarge their application in various fields has received increasing attention [20]. Thus, the development of surface-different C-dots with high quantum yield is still an interesting challenge.

Currently, some organic small molecules containing hydroxy or carboxyl group have been used for the synthesis of highly photoluminescent CDs [15,21,22]. dl-Malic acid, as a green and inexpensive reagent, possesses a hydroxy-group and two carboxyl-groups and these may render it ideal molecular precursor for CDs.

In this work, CDs were synthesized through the microwave-assisted pyrolysis from dl-malic acid with ethylenediamine. The fluorescence quantum yield was determined to be 16.0%, much higher than those reported in the literature [22–26]. Then we explored a fluorescent turn-off/on method using the prepared CDs as fluorescent probes to realize a simple, sensitive and selective analysis of guanine. The fluorescence intensity of CDs was quenched significantly due to nonradiative electron transfer when Cu2+ was added into the CD solution. Because of the coordination ability between Cu2+ ions and guanine, the fluorescence of the CDs was restored dramatically by the addition of guanine. Accordingly, the CDs are used as fluorescence probes for application in the detecting guanine. The obtained fluorescence sensing system has a low detection limit of 0.67×10−8 molL−1. Moreover, 100-fold adenine, thymine, cytosine and uracil have no interference for the detection of guanine. Comparing with those fluorescent probes for guanine [13,14], the sensing system exhibits high sensitivity and good selectivity. The proposed method could be used for determination of guanine in urine and DNA samples.

dl-Malic acid was obtained from Shanghai Yingpeng Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. (China). Ethylenediamine and copper sulfate were purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. (Beijing, China). Guanine was obtained from Beijing Qingshengda Chemical Technique Co., Ltd. (China). A stock solution of guanine (1.0×10−4 molL−1) was prepared and stored at 4°C. Working standard solution (3.3×10−6 molL−1) was freshly prepared by appropriate dilution of the stock solution before use. Tris–HCl buffer solutions with different pH values were prepared by dropwise added into a concentrated hydrochloric acid solution (0.1molL−1) or Tris solution (0.1molL−1) to required pH values. All the reagents were of analytical reagent.

The fluorescent spectra were recorded on a FP-6500 fluorescence spectrophotometer (Hitachi, Tokyo, Japan). The fluorescence quantum yield (QY) and fluorescence lifetime were measured by a FLS-980 fluorescent spectrophotometer (Edinburgh Instrument, UK). The UV–vis absorption spectra were obtained with a UV-1700 UV spectrophotometer (Shimadzu, Co., Japan). A high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) image was taken on a HITACHI H-8100 transmission electron microscopy (Hitachi, Tokyo, Japan) at an accelerating voltage of 200kV. The sample for HRTEM was prepared by evaporating a drop of the aqueous product on a cooper HRTEM grid and drying under room temperature. The 13C NMR spectrum was collected with a Bruker Avance 400MHz spectrometer (Bruker, Switzerland) by dissolving CDs in deuterated water. Fourier Transform Infrared spectrum (FTIR) in the range of 4000–400cm−1 was recorded in KBr pellets on a Nicolet FTS NEXUS FTIR spectrometer (Nicolet Instrument Co., USA) for evaluating the encapsulation of CDs. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) measurements were performed by an ESCALAB 250 XPS system (Thermo Scientific). The pH measurements were made with a pHS-3C digital acidity meter (Shanghai Dazhong Instrument Co., China).

CDs were synthesized as follows: dl-malic acid (1.0g) and ethylenediamine (0.5mL) were added to distilled water (10mL) to form a transparent solution. The mixture was heated in a domestic microwave oven (800W) for 7min to obtain dark solid. The material received was dispersed in water (10mL) and centrifuged at 12,000rpm for 15min to remove large particles, then filtrated by 0.2μm millipore filtration. To remove the nonreacted ions and small particles, the resultant solution was dialyzed against ultrapure water through a dialysis membrane (MWCO=3.5kDa) for 24h. At last, the purified CDs solution was dried by evaporation of water at 70°C in a vacuum oven for 24h. The resulting powder was dispersed in distilled water at a concentration of 1.0mgmL−1 for further characterization and use.

1.0mL of CDs from the stock dispersion, 0.7mL of Tris–HCl buffer solution (pH 7), 1.0mL of 8.0×10−5 molL−1 Cu2+ and appropriate amount of guanine working solution or sample solution were added into a 10mL volumetric flask. After mixed and diluted to the mark, the dispersion was heated in a boiling water bath for 5min, then, cooled to room temperature by running water. Finally, the fluorescent intensity was recorded from 475 to 600nm under the excitation wavelength at 451nm. The enhanced fluorescence intensity was denoted as ΔF = F − F 0, where F and F 0 were the intensities of system with and without guanine.

TEM image in Fig. 1A reveals CDs to be spherical and well distributed in water. The size histogram (Fig. 1B) shows a relatively narrow particle size distribution ranging from 0.6 to 1.6nm, with the average diameter of 1.02±0.04nm, which are smaller than the previously reported CDs [15,22,23,27]. HRTEM image (Fig. 1C) shows certain crystallinity with a lattice space of 0.34nm, which corresponds to the [002] facet of graphitic carbon [28].

The FTIR spectrum was recorded in order to further witness structural insights about the prepared CDs, as shown in Fig. S1A. The characteristic absorption bands of some functional groups is observed, including the stretching vibration of COH (3305cm−1), CH (3087cm−1, 2944cm−1) and CN (1400cm−1), the peak of CO group (1658cm−1) and the bending vibration of NH (1547cm−1) and CH (1185cm−1, 1089cm−1).

The FTIR spectrum was recorded in order to further witness structural insights about the prepared CDs, as shown in Fig. S1A. The characteristic absorption bands of some functional groups is observed, including the stretching vibration of COH (3305cm−1), CH (3087cm−1, 2944cm−1) and CN (1400cm−1), the peak of CO group (1658cm−1) and the bending vibration of NH (1547cm−1) and CH (1185cm−1, 1089cm−1).

The elemental analysis and surface composition for the resultant CDs were characterized by the XPS technique. Results showed that the as-prepared CDs contain C, N and O three elements, at relative percentage composition 73.03%, 4.07% and 22.91%, respectively, indicating the presence of carbon as the major component along with other minor components like nitrogen and oxygen. The three peaks at about 288.3, 405.0, 534.6eV shown in the XPS full scan spectrum (Fig. 2 A) can be attributed to C1s, N1s and O1s, respectively. The C1S spectrum (Fig. 2B) can be deconvoluted into three peaks with binding energies at 284.6, 285.8 and 288.3eV, corresponding to CC, CN and CO, respectively. The deconvoluted O1s spectrum (Fig. 2C) showed two speaks at about 531.8 and 533.6eV, corresponding to CO and COH/COC bands, respectively. The deconvoluted N1s spectrum (Fig. 2D) exhibits a peak at 398.9eV corresponding to CNC and a peak at 399.8eV corresponding to N(C)3 groups. All the results from the FTIR spectrum and XPS studies confirm that the surface of the as-prepared CDs is functionalized by multiple oxygen and nitrogen groups and that CDs can exist steadily in aqueous solution owing to a large amount of hydrophilic groups on the surface of the CDs.

The carbon groups were also investigated by 13C NMR. Three kinds of carbon peaks are exhibited in Fig. S1B. Signals in the range of 30–48ppm express aliphatic (sp3) carbon atoms. Signals located in 100–185ppm reveal sp2 carbon atoms. Signals from 167 to 185ppm correspond to carboxyl/amide groups.

The carbon groups were also investigated by 13C NMR. Three kinds of carbon peaks are exhibited in Fig. S1B. Signals in the range of 30–48ppm express aliphatic (sp3) carbon atoms. Signals located in 100–185ppm reveal sp2 carbon atoms. Signals from 167 to 185ppm correspond to carboxyl/amide groups.

UV–vis absorption spectrum of CDs is shown in Fig. S1C. The strong absorption peak at 245nm indicates a π→π* electronic transitions of CDs. The shoulder peak at 350nm reflects a n→π* transition of CO on the surface of CDs. Fig. 3 A depicts the fluorescence emission spectra of CDs. The maximum emission intensity is observed at 524nm when the CDs are excited at 451nm. The emission peaks position shifts from 460 to 540nm with excitation ranging from 350 to 470nm, revealing excitation-dependent emission behavior of the as-prepared CDs, which may be due to the different distribution of emissive traps, different surface states and size dispersion of CDs [29]. In addition, multicolor emission property of the CDs at different excitations is observed, as shown in Fig. 3B. A broader emission color ranges from blue, yellow and green to red with the excitation wavelength from the ultraviolet to visible region. The results imply the as-prepared CDs can be useful for multicolor imaging application. The multicolor emission property is associated to the optical selection of CDs with different sizes [30]. The quantum yield of the CDs was measured by a FLS-980 fluorescent spectrophotometer using an integrating sphere, and calculated to be 16.0%, which is higher than those produced by microwave-assisted synthesis methods [22–26].

UV–vis absorption spectrum of CDs is shown in Fig. S1C. The strong absorption peak at 245nm indicates a π→π* electronic transitions of CDs. The shoulder peak at 350nm reflects a n→π* transition of CO on the surface of CDs. Fig. 3A depicts the fluorescence emission spectra of CDs. The maximum emission intensity is observed at 524nm when the CDs are excited at 451nm. The emission peaks position shifts from 460 to 540nm with excitation ranging from 350 to 470nm, revealing excitation-dependent emission behavior of the as-prepared CDs, which may be due to the different distribution of emissive traps, different surface states and size dispersion of CDs [29]. In addition, multicolor emission property of the CDs at different excitations is observed, as shown in Fig. 3B. A broader emission color ranges from blue, yellow and green to red with the excitation wavelength from the ultraviolet to visible region. The results imply the as-prepared CDs can be useful for multicolor imaging application. The multicolor emission property is associated to the optical selection of CDs with different sizes [30]. The quantum yield of the CDs was measured by a FLS-980 fluorescent spectrophotometer using an integrating sphere, and calculated to be 16.0%, which is higher than those produced by microwave-assisted synthesis methods [22–26].

Moreover, the photoluminescence of the prepared CDs is also pH-dependent, as shown in Fig. S2A. The fluorescence intensity is proportional to pH in the range of 1–4, which suggests a potential application of CDs as a pH sensor. Additionally, fluorescence intensity drops gradually with the pH increasing from 4 to 10. The reason may be that, in strong acid condition, the protonation of carboxyl groups on the surface of CDs induces the aggregation of CDs [31]; At higher pH value, the deprotonation of carboxyl groups leads to form negative charges on the surface of CDs [25].

Moreover, the photoluminescence of the prepared CDs is also pH-dependent, as shown in Fig. S2A. The fluorescence intensity is proportional to pH in the range of 1–4, which suggests a potential application of CDs as a pH sensor. Additionally, fluorescence intensity drops gradually with the pH increasing from 4 to 10. The reason may be that, in strong acid condition, the protonation of carboxyl groups on the surface of CDs induces the aggregation of CDs [31]; At higher pH value, the deprotonation of carboxyl groups leads to form negative charges on the surface of CDs [25].

The as-prepared CDs exhibit good stability under different conditions. First, the fluorescence intensity of the CDs remains constant under continuous irradiation with a Xe lamp for 1h (Fig. S2B), indicating excellent photostability of the CDs. Second, the fluorescence intensity decreases less than 5% with the increase of NaCl concentration ranging from 0 to 1.0molL−1 (Fig. S2C), indicating good stability in high-salinity, which provides essential condition for CDs to be used in the presence of physical salt concentrations. Third, the fluorescence intensity decreases as temperature increases (Fig. S2D), displaying the temperature-dependent PL properties of CDs solution (inset in Fig. S2D, curve a), which was due to the fact that non-radiative transition probability increases with rising temperature. When CDs solution is returned to room temperature, the fluorescence is recovered (inset in Fig. S2D, line b), exhibiting remarkable stability in high temperature environment and suggesting great potential for the CDs to be used in the fabrication of temperature-sensitive devices. Forth, fluorescence intensity of the CDs remains almost unchanged even after 6 months in air at room temperature, exhibiting excellent stability. Given the above stabilities, CDs are beneficial for practical applications. The decay curves of the as-prepared CDs fitted well to double-exponential kinetics with a fast component (τ 1 3.47ns, ca. 33.03%) and a slow component (τ 1 8.39ns, ca. 66.97%), demonstrating non-uniform fluorescence radiative processes. According to
(1)
τ =
A 1
τ 1
+
A 2
τ 2
A 1
+
A 2
the average fluorescence lifetime of the CDs was calculated to be 6.76ns. Given the above stabilities and nanosecond lifetime, CDs are suitable for biological and optoelectronic applications.

The as-prepared CDs exhibit good stability under different conditions. First, the fluorescence intensity of the CDs remains constant under continuous irradiation with a Xe lamp for 1h (Fig. S2B), indicating excellent photostability of the CDs. Second, the fluorescence intensity decreases less than 5% with the increase of NaCl concentration ranging from 0 to 1.0molL−1 (Fig. S2C), indicating good stability in high-salinity, which provides essential condition for CDs to be used in the presence of physical salt concentrations. Third, the fluorescence intensity decreases as temperature increases (Fig. S2D), displaying the temperature-dependent PL properties of CDs solution (inset in Fig. S2D, curve a), which was due to the fact that non-radiative transition probability increases with rising temperature. When CDs solution is returned to room temperature, the fluorescence is recovered (inset in Fig. S2D, line b), exhibiting remarkable stability in high temperature environment and suggesting great potential for the CDs to be used in the fabrication of temperature-sensitive devices. Forth, fluorescence intensity of the CDs remains almost unchanged even after 6 months in air at room temperature, exhibiting excellent stability. Given the above stabilities, CDs are beneficial for practical applications. The decay curves of the as-prepared CDs fitted well to double-exponential kinetics with a fast component (τ 1 3.47ns, ca. 33.03%) and a slow component (τ 1 8.39ns, ca. 66.97%), demonstrating non-uniform fluorescence radiative processes. According to
(1)
τ =
A 1
τ 1
+
A 2
τ 2
A 1
+
A 2
the average fluorescence lifetime of the CDs was calculated to be 6.76ns. Given the above stabilities and nanosecond lifetime, CDs are suitable for biological and optoelectronic applications.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Response of CDs to metal ions
ce:section.ce:para: In order to test the response of CDs to metal ions, the selectivity of the CDs toward other metal cations such as Ag+, Cr3+, Zn2+, Pb2+, Co2+, Cu2+, Al3+, Ni2+, Fe2+, Mn2+, Hg2+ and Ca2+ is examined at the concentrations of 1.0×10−3 molL−1. As shown in Fig. 4A, no change in the fluorescence intensity of the CDs is observed after addition of Co2+, Ca2+, and Ag+ into the CDs solution. Addition of Cr3+, Zn2+, Pb2+, Al3+, Ni2+, Fe2+, Mn2+, Hg2+ slightly quenched the fluorescence intensity of the CDs. For Zn2+, Pb2+, Fe2+, and Hg2+, the slight fluorescence quenching can be ascribed to the nonspecific interactions between the carboxylic groups and the metal ions [21]. Compared with the metal ions mentioned above, Cu2+ shows the most obvious quenching on the prepared CDs. The fluorescence quenching was confirmed to be a dynamic quenching (see the quenching mechanism in supporting information), which may be ascribed to nonradiative electron transfer from the excited-state CDs to the d-orbital of Cu2+.

Encouraged by the fluorescence quenching of Cu2+ on CDs and considering the coordination of guanine to Cu2+ [32,33], we then explore the response of CDs–Cu2+ to guanine and the other bases. The same level of adenine, guanine, cytosine, uracil and thymine concentration was added into the CDs–Cu2+ system, as shown in Fig. 4B. Clearly, only guanine recovers the fluorescence intensity of the CDs. Other species show almost no fluorescence enhancement effect. The results confirm that CDs–Cu2+ exhibits a good selectivity toward guanine over the other bases, which can be ascribed to the binding of guanine with Cu2+ (see Section 3.5.1 discussed below).

Fig. 5 shows the fluorescence emission spectra of CDs–Cu2+–guanine system. Obviously, CDs exhibit a strong fluorescence emission band centered at 524nm by fixing the excitation wavelength at 451nm. Guanine has a little effect on the fluorescence of the CDs. With the addition of Cu2+ into the CDs, the fluorescence intensity decreases significantly, which may result from the nonradiative electron transfer process between CDs and Cu2+. However, with the addition of guanine into the mixture of CDs–Cu2+, the fluorescence intensity is recovered apparently. The reason for this phenomenon is that Cu2+ is bidentate with guanine N7 and O6 [32,33]. In order to further confirm the binding between Cu2+ and guanine, the absorption spectra of Cu2+, guanine and Cu2+–guanine were recorded. As shown in Fig. 6 , a weak absorbance band centered at around 255nm (B band) was observed in the spectrum of guanine in the absence of Cu2+ ion, and this low absorption is attributed to the π→π* electron transition. However, once a trace amount of Cu2+(8μM) was added into the solution of guanine, the absorption peak of guanine disappeared dramatically, indicating the binding of guanine with Cu2+. On the basis of the above considerations, the mechanism of the “off–on” detection of guanine using CDs as fluorescence probes in the presence of Cu2+ are shown in Scheme 1 . Additionally, upon addition of guanine into CDs–Cu2+, a gradual fluorescence enhancement and a color change were observed (Fig. 5). Therefore, the CDs–Cu(II) system can serve as a “turn-on” fluorescent sensor for determination of guanine.

To achieve a highest possible sensitivity for guanine detection, the experimental conditions were optimized prior to the guanine assay. The effects of five buffer solutions (KH2PO4–NaOH, KH2PO4–borax, NaH2PO4–Na2HPO4, citric acid–Na2HPO4 and Tris–HCl) were studied in the present experiments. Among them Tris–HCl was found to be the best buffer medium because of giving the highest ΔF. It is well known that the pH of the medium could affect detection sensitivity. So, the effects of pH and dose of Tris–HCl buffer solution on sensitivity were investigated (Fig. S3A, B). When 0.7mL of the buffer solution with pH 7 was used, ΔF reached highest. Hence, 0.7mL of pH 7 Tris–HCl buffer solution was selected in this work. The concentration of CDs should be monitored carefully because CDs act as a fluorescent reagent. As shown in Fig. S3C, when the concentration of CDs was higher than 0.09mgmL−1, ΔF reached a maximum and remained constant. Hence, 0.1mgmL−1 CDs was used for the subsequent experiments. It was speculated that the concentration of Cu2+ plays an important part in the investigated system because Cu2+ serves dual roles as a quenching reagent to reduce the fluorescence of CDs and central ion to form Cu2+–guanine complex. Thus, the effect of Cu2+ concentration in the range 2.4×10−6 to 9.6×10−6 molL−1 on this assay was investigated (Fig. S3D). It was found that Cu2+ solution at a concentration of 8.0×10−6 molL−1 was the most suitable due to giving the highest ΔF. As we know, temperature has a great effect on dynamic quenching process and complex formation. As displayed in Fig. S3E, F 0 decreased with increasing temperature due to the fact that raising temperature is benefit to dynamic quenching process. While F kept constant when the temperature is raised from 20 to 100°C, which indicates that the complex of Cu2+–guanine exhibit good thermal stability. Consequently, enhanced fluorescence intensity (ΔF) increased gradually with the increase of temperature. Therefore, boiling water was chosen for this system. In addition, ΔF gave a maximum and kept constant when heating time was ≥4min (Fig. S3F), indicating that it is very fast to complete the reaction. In this work, 5min was chosen as reaction time for the further studied. In order to investigate the effect of CDs size on the sensor properties, the CDs of different size were obtained by changing centrifugation speed [34]. Fig. S4 indicated that the relatively small size of CDs exhibits a relatively high sensitivity for the detection of guanine, which may be ascribed to varying of the optical properties of CDs with size [34]. Under the optimized conditions mentioned above, the fluorescence intensity of the CDs–Cu2+–guanine system remains constant for at least 3.0h at room temperature.

To achieve a highest possible sensitivity for guanine detection, the experimental conditions were optimized prior to the guanine assay. The effects of five buffer solutions (KH2PO4–NaOH, KH2PO4–borax, NaH2PO4–Na2HPO4, citric acid–Na2HPO4 and Tris–HCl) were studied in the present experiments. Among them Tris–HCl was found to be the best buffer medium because of giving the highest ΔF. It is well known that the pH of the medium could affect detection sensitivity. So, the effects of pH and dose of Tris–HCl buffer solution on sensitivity were investigated (Fig. S3A, B). When 0.7mL of the buffer solution with pH 7 was used, ΔF reached highest. Hence, 0.7mL of pH 7 Tris–HCl buffer solution was selected in this work. The concentration of CDs should be monitored carefully because CDs act as a fluorescent reagent. As shown in Fig. S3C, when the concentration of CDs was higher than 0.09mgmL−1, ΔF reached a maximum and remained constant. Hence, 0.1mgmL−1 CDs was used for the subsequent experiments. It was speculated that the concentration of Cu2+ plays an important part in the investigated system because Cu2+ serves dual roles as a quenching reagent to reduce the fluorescence of CDs and central ion to form Cu2+–guanine complex. Thus, the effect of Cu2+ concentration in the range 2.4×10−6 to 9.6×10−6 molL−1 on this assay was investigated (Fig. S3D). It was found that Cu2+ solution at a concentration of 8.0×10−6 molL−1 was the most suitable due to giving the highest ΔF. As we know, temperature has a great effect on dynamic quenching process and complex formation. As displayed in Fig. S3E, F 0 decreased with increasing temperature due to the fact that raising temperature is benefit to dynamic quenching process. While F kept constant when the temperature is raised from 20 to 100°C, which indicates that the complex of Cu2+–guanine exhibit good thermal stability. Consequently, enhanced fluorescence intensity (ΔF) increased gradually with the increase of temperature. Therefore, boiling water was chosen for this system. In addition, ΔF gave a maximum and kept constant when heating time was ≥4min (Fig. S3F), indicating that it is very fast to complete the reaction. In this work, 5min was chosen as reaction time for the further studied. In order to investigate the effect of CDs size on the sensor properties, the CDs of different size were obtained by changing centrifugation speed [34]. Fig. S4 indicated that the relatively small size of CDs exhibits a relatively high sensitivity for the detection of guanine, which may be ascribed to varying of the optical properties of CDs with size [34]. Under the optimized conditions mentioned above, the fluorescence intensity of the CDs–Cu2+–guanine system remains constant for at least 3.0h at room temperature.

Under the aforementioned conditions, the standard curve for detection of guanine was obtained between guanine concentration (c) and enhanced fluorescence intensity (ΔF) (Fig. 7 ). It was found that. ΔF is proportional to the concentration of guanine in the range of 1.31×10−8 to 7.27×10−7 molL−1 with the regression equation ΔF =−1.48+338.15c (μM) (R =0.9992, n =12). The limit of detection for guanine was 0.67×10−8 molL−1 at a signal-to-noise ratio of 3, which is lower than that of fluorescent probes based on thioglycolic acid-capped CdTe nanoparticles [13] and nuclear fast red [14]. To demonstrate the repeatability of the proposed assay system, eleven repetitive measurements on the same sample were carried out, and the relative standard deviation (RSD) for 6.62×10−8 molL−1 guanine was calculated to be 1.9%. These results demonstrate that the present method has good linearity, relatively high sensitivity and precision.

In order to assess the selectivity of the proposed method, the effects of some metal ions, non-metallic ions, carbohydrates, inorganic acid radicals and nucleobases on the determination of 6.62×10−8 molL−1 guanine were tested. When the tolerance limit was taken as the concentration of foreign substances causing a relative error less than 5.0% in fluorescence intensity, the tolerance ratios are shown in Table 1 . The results demonstrate that this assay presents a good selectivity toward guanine. The developed method is good, when compared to other fluorescent probe methods described in the literature [13,14] for the determination of guanine in terms of interference effect of species.

To evaluate the potentials of the present procedure's applicability in real samples, we used the prepared CDs as fluorescence probes to sense guanine in human urine and herring sperm DNA. Appropriate amount of urine samples was spiked with guanine, and assayed by standard curve method. The thermally denaturated herring sperm DNA samples were prepared according to the previous report [35]. In short, DNA samples were dissolved in redistilled deionized water and heated in a boiling water bath for 10min, then the samples were cooled with ice water rapidly. The content of guanine in herring sperm DNA was determined by standard addition method. The results are given in Table 2 . The recoveries were from 98.1% to 103.0% and RSD was 0.9–2.6%, confirming that the proposed method can be applied to practical samples with high accuracy and good repeatability.

In summary, we have developed a green, economical and facile method to synthesize fluorescence nanodots by a one-step microwave pyrolysis approach with dl-malic acid and ethylenediamine as the precursors. The prepared CDs exhibit a relatively narrow particle size distribution, high quantum yield, and excellent stability. Moreover, a “turn-on” fluorescent sensor CDs–Cu(II) for the detection of guanine has been described. The proposed sensing system possesses excellent analytical performance, such as high selectivity and sensitivity, low detection limit, wide linear response range, considerable time saving and low cost. The method reported here has been used to detect guanine in urine and DNA samples. The present work not only enriches the determination of purine compounds, but also manifests the potential applications of CDs in fluorescence sensing.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.snb.2015.09.037.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following are the supplementary data to this article:

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Fast simultaneous determination of 14 nucleosides and nucleobases in cultured Cordyceps using ultra-performance liquid chromatography
Electrochemical behaviors and simultaneous determination of guanine and adenine based on graphene-ionic liquid-chitosan composite film modified glassy carbon electrode
A glassy carbon electrode modified with electrochemically reduced graphene for simultaneous determination of guanine and adenine
Simultaneous determination of guanine and adenine in DNA using an electrochemically pretreated glassy carbon electrode
CeO2 nanoparticles decorated multi-walled carbon nanotubes for electrochemical determination of guanine and adenine
Simultaneous determination of guanine in human plasma using high-performance liquid chromatography
Simultaneous determination of guanine, uric acid, hypoxanthine and xanthine in human plasma by reversed-phase high-performance liquid chromatography with amperometric detection
Adsorption-voltammetric determination of guanine, guanosine, adenine and adenosine with capillary zone electrophoresis separation
Separation of six purine bases by capillary electrophoresis with electrochemical detection
Determination of adenine and guanine by capillary zone electrophoresis with end-column amperometric detection at a carbon fiber microdisk array electrode
Synthesis-modification integration: one-step fabrication of boronic acid functionalized carbon dots for fluorescent blood sugar sensing
Facile aqueous synthesis of functionalized CdTe nanoparticles and their application as fluorescence probes for determination of adenine and guanine
Nuclear fast red as highly sensitive “off/on” fluorescent probe for detecting guanine
Water-soluble and phosphorus-containing carbon dots with strong green fluorescence for cell labeling
N-doped carbon quantum dots for TiO2-based photocatalysts and dye-sensitized solar cells
White light-emitting devices based on carbon dots’ electroluminescence
Carbon nanodots: synthesis, properties and applications
Highly luminescent carbon nanodots by microwave-assisted pyrolysis
Ionic liquids as precursors for highly luminescent, surface-different nitrogen-doped carbon dots used for label-free detection of Cu2+/Fe3+ and cell imaging
Highly photoluminescent carbon dots for multicolor patterning, sensors, and bioimaging
One-step synthesis of surface passivated carbon nanodots by microwave assisted pyrolysis for enhanced multicolor photoluminescence and bioimaging
Microwave heating of arginine yields highly fluorescent nanoparticles
Microwave assisted one-step green synthesis of fluorescent carbon nanoparticles from ionic liquids and their application as novel fluorescence probe for quercetin determination
Microwave-assisted rapid green synthesis of photoluminescent carbon nanodots from flour and their applications for sensitive and selective detection of mercury(II) ions
Microwave-assisted synthesis of carbon nanodots through an eggshell membrane and their fluorescent application
One-pot hydrothermal synthesis of highly luminescent nitrogen-doped amphoteric carbon dots for bioimaging from Bombyx mori silk – natural proteins
One-step ultrasonic synthesis of fluorescent N-doped carbon dots from glucose and their visible-light sensitive photocatalytic ability
A facile and versatile approach to biocompatible “fluorescent polymers” from polymerizable carbon nanodots
Enhanced photoluminescence and characterization of multicolor carbon dots using plant soot as a carbon source
One-pot green synthesis of optically pH-sensitive carbon dots with upconversion luminescence
Determination of nanomolar levels of guanine by differential-pulse adsorptive cathodic stripping voltammetry of its copper(II) complex
Deprotonation studies of Cu+–guanine and Cu2+–guanine complexes by theoretical investigation
Simple one-step synthesis of highly luminescent carbon dots from orange juice: application as excellent bio-imaging agents
Direct electrocatalytic oxidation of adenine and guanine on carbon ionic liquid electrode and the simultaneous determination

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Shengbin Pang received his BS degree from School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering at Henan Normal University (PR China) in 2012. Now he is pursuing his MS study under the supervision of Prof. Suling Feng. His research interests focus on the design and synthesis of carbon dots for guanine and metal ions.

Yi Zhang is a lecturer in the School of Laboratory Medicine at Xinxiang Medical University. She received her MS degree from School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering at Henan Normal University (PR China) in 2009. Her current research interests are the synthesis of nanomaterials and their applications in various fields.

Chengke Wu is a lecturer in the School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering at Henan Normal University (PR China). He received her PhD degree from Sun-Yat University in 2010. His current research interests are electroanalytical chemistry and the synthesis of nanomaterials-based fluorescent probes.

Suling Feng is a professor in the School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering at Henan Normal University (PR China). She received her PhD degree from Lanzhou University in 2005. Her main research interests involve nanomaterials synthesis and modification, and design nanomaterials-based fluorescent probes.
